IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles the important task of end-to-end computational argumentation mining and reports new state-of-the-art results on the Persuasive Essays (PE) dataset, which is likely to influence model selection for this area. By demonstrating that simpler BiLSTM-CRF based tagging models robustly capture long-range dependencies and that token-based dependency parsing underperforms, it should change how researchers frame the task and prioritize approaches. The finding that multi-task learning of natural subtasks improves end-to-end performance opens useful research directions. Impact is somewhat constrained by evaluation on a single dataset and the omission of other framings (e.g., seq2seq), so I judge the overall influence as strong but not transformative. | B(4): The paper tackles the important problem of end-to-end computational argumentation mining and reports new state-of-the-art results on the Persuasive Essays (PE) dataset, comparing extensively against ILP, several dependency parsers, and neural baselines (LSTM-ER, BiLSTM-CRF-CNN). Its empirical findings—that simpler token-based BiLSTM-CRF tagging models are robust, token-based dependency parsing performs poorly for this task, and multi-task learning of subtasks improves end-to-end performance—are likely to influence model choices and evaluation practices in the area. Impact is tempered by evaluation on a single dataset and by unexplored framings (e.g., seq2seq), so the contribution is strong but not necessarily transformative across NLP more broadly.

By demonstrating that simpler BiLSTM-CRF based tagging models robustly capture long-range dependencies and that token-based dependency parsing underperforms, it should change how researchers frame the task and prioritize approaches. The finding that multi-task learning of natural subtasks improves end-to-end performance opens useful research directions. Impact is somewhat constrained by evaluation on a single dataset and the omission of other framings (e.g., seq2seq), so I judge the overall influence as strong but not transformative.

Its empirical findings—that simpler token-based BiLSTM-CRF tagging models are robust, token-based dependency parsing performs poorly for this task, and multi-task learning of subtasks improves end-to-end performance—are likely to influence model choices and evaluation practices in the area. Impact is tempered by evaluation on a single dataset and by unexplored framings (e.g., seq2seq), so the contribution is strong but not necessarily transformative across NLP more broadly.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper applies existing neural architectures (BiLSTM-CRF variants, LSTM-ER) to end-to-end argumentation mining and empirically compares framing choices (token-based dependency parsing vs. sequence tagging) on the Persuasive Essays dataset, achieving state-of-the-art results. While the systematic evaluation and multi-task tagging insights are useful, the core methods are adaptations of well-known models rather than fundamentally new algorithms or theoretical perspectives. The contribution is therefore best characterized as an incremental, empirical advancement that clarifies which existing techniques work well for this task. | B(3): The paper applies and adapts existing neural sequence models (BiLSTM-CRF variants, LSTM-ER) to end-to-end argumentation mining and systematically compares several framings (token-based dependency parsing vs sequence tagging) on the Persuasive Essays dataset, achieving new SOTA. While the explicit comparison of framings and the finding that token-based dependency parsing is suboptimal are useful insights, the core methods are adaptations of well-known neural techniques and multi-task learning rather than fundamentally new algorithms. Given the comprehensive empirical evaluation and clear differentiation from prior ILP/parser baselines, the contribution is moderately novel but largely incremental.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper applies existing neural architectures (BiLSTM-CRF variants, LSTM-ER) to end-to-end argumentation mining and empirically compares framing choices (token-based dependency parsing vs. sequence tagging) on the Persuasive Essays dataset, achieving state-of-the-art results. While the systematic evaluation and multi-task tagging insights are useful, the core methods are adaptations of well-known models rather than fundamentally new algorithms or theoretical perspectives. The contribution is therefore best characterized as an incremental, empirical advancement that clarifies which existing techniques work well for this task.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper applies and adapts existing neural sequence models (BiLSTM-CRF variants, LSTM-ER) to end-to-end argumentation mining and systematically compares several framings (token-based dependency parsing vs sequence tagging) on the Persuasive Essays dataset, achieving new SOTA. While the explicit comparison of framings and the finding that token-based dependency parsing is suboptimal are useful insights, the core methods are adaptations of well-known neural techniques and multi-task learning rather than fundamentally new algorithms. APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): Yes — the paper addresses computational argumentation mining, a core NLP topic, and evaluates on the standard Persuasive Essays (PE) dataset (Stab and Gurevych, 2016). The methods (BiLSTM-CRF variants, BiLSTM tagging, LSTM-ER, comparison to feature-based ILP and several dependency parsers) and the reported evaluation metrics (component F1, relation F1, Persing & Ng-style span matching) match expectations for a top NLP/ML venue. The work is positioned within the appropriate community (thorough related work coverage, comparisons to prior SOTA) and targets the right audience, with only minor scope extensions (e.g., encoder-decoder framings) left for future work. | B(5): This paper addresses computational argumentation mining using modern neural techniques (BiLSTM-CRF variants, LSTM-ER) and compares to strong baselines (feature-based ILP, dependency parsers, prior LSTM-ER), which aligns well with top NLP/ML conference topics. It uses standard dataset (Persuasive Essays) and expected evaluation metrics (component and relation F1, span matching), positions itself relative to relevant prior work, and evaluates multiple model framings, making it a strong fit for the venue. The focus on end-to-end neural modeling and multi-task setups targets the right audience and methodological expectations for top-tier NLP/ML conferences.

reviewer_a.justification: Yes — the paper addresses computational argumentation mining, a core NLP topic, and evaluates on the standard Persuasive Essays (PE) dataset (Stab and Gurevych, 2016). The methods (BiLSTM-CRF variants, BiLSTM tagging, LSTM-ER, comparison to feature-based ILP and several dependency parsers) and the reported evaluation metrics (component F1, relation F1, Persing & Ng-style span matching) match expectations for a top NLP/ML venue. The work is positioned within the appropriate community (thorough related work coverage, comparisons to prior SOTA) and targets the right audience, with only minor scope extensions (e.g., encoder-decoder framings) left for future work.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses computational argumentation mining using modern neural techniques (BiLSTM-CRF variants, LSTM-ER) and compares to strong baselines (feature-based ILP, dependency parsers, prior LSTM-ER), which aligns well with top NLP/ML conference topics. It uses standard dataset (Persuasive Essays) and expected evaluation metrics (component and relation F1, span matching), positions itself relative to relevant prior work, and evaluates multiple model framings, making it a strong fit for the venue. The focus on end-to-end neural modeling and multi-task setups targets the right audience and methodological expectations for top-tier NLP/ML conferences.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The experiments are reasonably comprehensive in model variety and baselines (BiLSTM-CRF variants, LSTM-ER, multiple dependency parsers, and the feature-based ILP) and use standard evaluation metrics on the Persuasive Essays (PE) dataset, supporting the main claims about strong neural and multi-task models. However, the paper lacks reported hyperparameters and code (harming reproducibility), does not report ablation or sensitivity analyses, and evaluates on a single dataset; while some negative results and limitations (poor token-based dependency parsing performance, omission of encoder-decoder framing) are discussed, the missing ablations and reproducibility details are important gaps. | B(3): The experiments compare a broad set of neural models (BiLSTM-CRF, BiLSTM-CRF-CNN, LSTM-ER), multiple dependency parsers, and the original feature-based ILP baseline on the Persuasive Essays (PE) dataset and report appropriate component/relation F1 metrics, which supports many of the claims. However, no ablation studies or sensitivity analyses are mentioned, hyperparameters are not reported and code availability is not stated, limiting reproducibility. The work also appears limited to a single dataset and explicitly leaves alternative framings (e.g., encoder–decoder) to future work, so some important experiments are missing to fully validate generality.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments are reasonably comprehensive in model variety and baselines (BiLSTM-CRF variants, LSTM-ER, multiple dependency parsers, and the feature-based ILP) and use standard evaluation metrics on the Persuasive Essays (PE) dataset, supporting the main claims about strong neural and multi-task models. However, the paper lacks reported hyperparameters and code (harming reproducibility), does not report ablation or sensitivity analyses, and evaluates on a single dataset; while some negative results and limitations (poor token-based dependency parsing performance, omission of encoder-decoder framing) are discussed, the missing ablations and reproducibility details are important gaps.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments compare a broad set of neural models (BiLSTM-CRF, BiLSTM-CRF-CNN, LSTM-ER), multiple dependency parsers, and the original feature-based ILP baseline on the Persuasive Essays (PE) dataset and report appropriate component/relation F1 metrics, which supports many of the claims. However, no ablation studies or sensitivity analyses are mentioned, hyperparameters are not reported and code availability is not stated, limiting reproducibility. The work also appears limited to a single dataset and explicitly leaves alternative framings (e.g., encoder–decoder) to future work, so some important experiments are missing to fully validate generality.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well-organized and easy to follow, with the main contributions and experimental setup stated early (e.g., SOTA claim on the PE dataset, comparisons to ILP, LSTM-ER, BiLSTM-CRF variants, and various parsers) and a clear methodology section describing tagger and parser framings. Evaluation metrics and baselines are explicitly listed, and related work coverage is thorough, supporting clarity of context. Minor presentation issues reduce completeness: hyperparameters are not reported and code availability is not mentioned, which hurts reproducibility and should be addressed. | B(4): The paper is generally well organized and clearly written (writing quality: good) with a thorough related-work section and clearly described methodology and evaluation setup (datasets, baselines, and metrics are explicitly listed). The main contributions are stated early (claims summarized) and limitations are acknowledged, which aids readability. A minor presentation issue is the lack of reported hyperparameters and no mention of code release, which reduces reproducibility and is why I did not rate it a perfect 5.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well-organized and easy to follow, with the main contributions and experimental setup stated early (e.g., SOTA claim on the PE dataset, comparisons to ILP, LSTM-ER, BiLSTM-CRF variants, and various parsers) and a clear methodology section describing tagger and parser framings. Evaluation metrics and baselines are explicitly listed, and related work coverage is thorough, supporting clarity of context. Minor presentation issues reduce completeness: hyperparameters are not reported and code availability is not mentioned, which hurts reproducibility and should be addressed.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is generally well organized and clearly written (writing quality: good) with a thorough related-work section and clearly described methodology and evaluation setup (datasets, baselines, and metrics are explicitly listed). The main contributions are stated early (claims summarized) and limitations are acknowledged, which aids readability. A minor presentation issue is the lack of reported hyperparameters and no mention of code release, which reduces reproducibility and is why I did not rate it a perfect 5.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares to appropriate and recent baselines including the feature-based ILP SOTA (Stab & Gurevych 2016), neural baselines (LSTM-ER, BiLSTM-CRF-CNN) and multiple dependency parsers, all evaluated on the same Persuasive Essays dataset with the same F1/span matching metrics. Comparisons appear fair and the paper analyzes differences between framings (token-based parsing vs tagging) and multi-task gains, and notes parsing performs subpar. Minor omissions reduce full marks: hyperparameter/code details are not reported in the summary, and it is not fully clear whether the authors explicitly acknowledge cases where prior neural baselines perform comparably to their method. | B(4): The paper compares against a broad set of appropriate baselines (feature-based ILP from Stab & Gurevych 2016, multiple dependency parsers, LSTM-ER, BiLSTM-CRF-CNN) on the same Persuasive Essays dataset using the same component/relation F1 and span-matching metrics. It analyzes alternative framings (token-based dependency parsing vs. tagging and multi-task tagging) and reports where certain framings perform poorly, and related work coverage is described as thorough. The summary does not explicitly state whether the authors acknowledge cases where prior methods perform comparably or better, so while comparisons are comprehensive and fair, that specific acknowledgment appears to be missing or not reported.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares to appropriate and recent baselines including the feature-based ILP SOTA (Stab & Gurevych 2016), neural baselines (LSTM-ER, BiLSTM-CRF-CNN) and multiple dependency parsers, all evaluated on the same Persuasive Essays dataset with the same F1/span matching metrics. Comparisons appear fair and the paper analyzes differences between framings (token-based parsing vs tagging) and multi-task gains, and notes parsing performs subpar. Minor omissions reduce full marks: hyperparameter/code details are not reported in the summary, and it is not fully clear whether the authors explicitly acknowledge cases where prior neural baselines perform comparably to their method.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against a broad set of appropriate baselines (feature-based ILP from Stab & Gurevych 2016, multiple dependency parsers, LSTM-ER, BiLSTM-CRF-CNN) on the same Persuasive Essays dataset using the same component/relation F1 and span-matching metrics. It analyzes alternative framings (token-based dependency parsing vs. tagging and multi-task tagging) and reports where certain framings perform poorly, and related work coverage is described as thorough. The summary does not explicitly state whether the authors acknowledge cases where prior methods perform comparably or better, so while comparisons are comprehensive and fair, that specific acknowledgment appears to be missing or not reported.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper’s main claims are backed by empirical comparisons against appropriate baselines (feature-based ILP from Stab and Gurevych 2016, multiple dependency parsers, LSTM-ER, and BiLSTM-CRF-CNN variants) on the Persuasive Essays dataset, supporting the SOTA and framing conclusions. There are no mathematical derivations to inspect, and the experimental setup is broadly sound in terms of baselines and tasks, but the summary indicates missing reproducibility details (no hyperparameters or code reported) and no mention of statistical significance testing. These omissions are minor methodological shortcomings that do not appear to invalidate the main experimental conclusions. | B(4): The paper's principal empirical claims (new state-of-the-art on the PE dataset; token-based dependency parsing performs worse; BiLSTM tagging models and multi-task learning are effective) are backed by experiments against appropriate baselines (feature-based ILP, multiple parsers, LSTM-ER, BiLSTM-CRF-CNN) using standard component/relation F1 metrics with Persing & Ng span matching. There are no mathematical derivations to critique and the technical comparisons appear sound, but key experimental details are missing (no hyperparameter reporting, no code mentioned) and there is no mention of statistical significance or evaluation beyond the single PE dataset, which limits reproducibility and generalizability. These are minor methodological shortcomings that do not invalidate the main conclusions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper’s main claims are backed by empirical comparisons against appropriate baselines (feature-based ILP from Stab and Gurevych 2016, multiple dependency parsers, LSTM-ER, and BiLSTM-CRF-CNN variants) on the Persuasive Essays dataset, supporting the SOTA and framing conclusions. There are no mathematical derivations to inspect, and the experimental setup is broadly sound in terms of baselines and tasks, but the summary indicates missing reproducibility details (no hyperparameters or code reported) and no mention of statistical significance testing. These omissions are minor methodological shortcomings that do not appear to invalidate the main experimental conclusions.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's principal empirical claims (new state-of-the-art on the PE dataset; token-based dependency parsing performs worse; BiLSTM tagging models and multi-task learning are effective) are backed by experiments against appropriate baselines (feature-based ILP, multiple parsers, LSTM-ER, BiLSTM-CRF-CNN) using standard component/relation F1 metrics with Persing & Ng span matching. There are no mathematical derivations to critique and the technical comparisons appear sound, but key experimental details are missing (no hyperparameter reporting, no code mentioned) and there is no mention of statistical significance or evaluation beyond the single PE dataset, which limits reproducibility and generalizability. These are minor methodological shortcomings that do not invalidate the main conclusions.

main_claims: The proposed neural end-to-end approach achieves new state-of-the-art results on the Persuasive Essays (PE) dataset from Stab and Gurevych (2016).
Framing argumentation mining as token-based dependency parsing yields subpar performance compared to alternative framings.
Less complex local tagging models based on BiLSTMs (including BiLSTM-CRF variants) perform robustly and can capture long-range dependencies in argumentation mining.
Jointly learning natural subtasks in a multi-task learning setup improves performance on the end-to-end argumentation mining task.
_summary.methodology: They investigate neural techniques for end-to-end argumentation mining by framing the task as both token-based dependency parsing and token-based sequence tagging (including multi-task tagging), employing BiLSTM-based tagging models (e.g., BiLSTM-CRF, BiLSTM-CRF-CNN) and adapting an LSTM-ER model for entity-and-relation extraction, and comparing these to feature-based ILP and parser baselines.
datasets: Persuasive Essays (PE) dataset from Stab and Gurevych (2016)
baselines: Dependency parsers: MST (McDonald et al., 2005), Mate (Bohnet and Nivre, 2012), LSTM-Parser (Dyer et al., 2015), Kiperwasser (Kiperwasser and Goldberg, 2016)
metrics: F1 computed from TP, FP, FN as in Persing and Ng (2016) (level-α matching for spans)
limitations: Token-based dependency parsing framing produced subpar performance for this task.
Encoder-decoder framing (e.g., Seq2Seq) was not considered/evaluated here and is left as scope for future work.
